content.full_text:
Suzan D. Hatfield
ATTORNEY AT LAW

220 NELLEN AVENUE
CORTE MADERA, CA 94925
(415) 924-4600

February 13, 1990


John Rothman
Legal Counsel, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Dear Mr. Rothman:

This letter follows our telephone conversation on February 9 in which we
discussed the disclosure requirements applicable to the District's elected
officials and employees under the Fair Political Practices Act. You have
advised me that those elected officials deemed to be "other public officials
who manage public investments" are required to file Form 721 as well as Form
730 because they are deemed to have assumed office effective January 1 of
1990. After this year, those officials will be required to file a Form 730
annually by April 1 covering the preceding year. In that regard, District
board members and employees deemed to be public officials managing public
investments are, as Article II officials, not subject to the District's own
Conflict of Interest Code, but to the state's reporting requirements only.

We discussed filing obligations of the rest of the District's designated
employees and you advised me that the FPPC has taken the position in the
Alperin matter (3 FPPC 79) that local agencies may not require local
officials and employees, pursuant to a local conflict of interest code, to
meet more stringent conflicts requirements than is required of Article II
employees. In that regard, therefore, designated employees of a local agency
other than those who are "public officials managing public investments" would
be deemed to be in compliance with Article III if the local agency were to
require them to annually file Form 730 so long as the local code did not
contain provisions which were more stringent than state law.

Please let me know if my view of the conclusions reached in our conversation
varies from your understanding of the law in this area.

I appreciate your assistance in this matter.

Sincerely,

Suzan D. Hatfield

SDH:eb

sections.question_synthetic: The letter confirms FPPC guidance regarding disclosure filing requirements for elected officials and employees under the Fair Political Practices Act, specifically regarding Form 721 and Form 730 filing obligations and the applicability of local conflict of interest codes to different classes of public officials.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request/correspondence letter from an attorney (Suzan D. Hatfield) seeking confirmation of FPPC guidance rather than an FPPC response letter. The document lacks formal QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections typical of FPPC advice letters. The letter references prior FPPC guidance on telephone and cites the Alperin case (3 FPPC 79). Document quality shows OCR artifacts (e.g., 'NELLEN AVENI'E', 'Fa'ir', 'off ic'ials').

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: The letter confirms FPPC guidance regarding disclosure filing requirements for elected officials and employees under the Fair Political Practices Act, specifically regarding Form 721 and Form 730 filing obligations and the applicability of local conflict of interest codes to different classes of public officials.

You have advised me that those elected officials deemed to be "other public officials who manage public investments" are required to file Form 721 as well as Form 730 because they are deemed to have assumed office effective January 1 of 1990. After this year, those officials will be required to file a Form 730 annually by April 1 covering the preceding year. In that regard, District board members and employees deemed to be public officials managing public investments are, as Article II officials, not subject to the District's own Conflict of Interest Code, but to the state's reporting requirements only. We discussed filing obligations of the rest of the District's designated employees and you advised me that the FPPC has taken the position in the Alperin matter (3 FPPC 79) that local agencies may not require local officials and employees, pursuant to a local conflict of interest code, to meet more stringent conflicts requirements than is required of Article II employees. In that regard, therefore, designated employees of a local agency other than those who are "public officials managing public investments" would be deemed to be in compliance with Article III if the local agency were to require them to annually file Form 730 so long as the local code did not contain provisions which were more stringent than state law. embedding.summary: This is a follow-up letter from an attorney to FPPC Legal Counsel summarizing guidance received during a telephone conversation regarding disclosure filing requirements for district officials and employees, particularly concerning Form 721/730 filings and the limitations on local conflict of interest codes under the Alperin precedent.
